figures.caption: Figure 1. The distribution-conditioned transport framework. A source distribution (teal) is pushed to a target distribution (purple) by a transport model T which is conditioned on distribution embeddings learned by an encoder E . The learned transport map is universal in the sense that any distribution can in principle be pushed to any distribution by conditioning on the corresponding source and target embeddings.
Table 1. Batch effect transfer quality on held-out samples. MMD distance (↓) averaged across all pairs of 3 held-out donors.
Table 8. Distributional distances (↓) for GMM experiments across K unique distributions. We report MMD, SWD, and Energy distance. IID Targets

Table 17. Distributional metrics (↓). Mean ± standard deviation reported for IID (replicate holdout) and OOD (patient holdout) settings.

Table 2. Perturbation prediction perfomance on PDO task. MMD distance ×102 (↓) averaged over held-out patients.

Table 21. TCR repertoire prediction performance across conditioning strategies (mean ± standard error). DFM shows meaningful conditioning (Oracle ≫ Predictor ≫ Within-patient), while ProGen2 shows little conditioning effect due to encoder collapse (all embeddings have cosine similarity > 0.998).
Table 20. Hyperparameters for TCR repertoire forecasting experiments.

Figure 6. Latent-space interpolants on the MVN any-to-any benchmark. Left: STC trajectory obtained by sequentially pushing samples using intermediate latent codes along a linear interpolant between endpoint embeddings. Right: OT displacement interpolation (computed in closed form for Gaussians).
